coredata.dc:title: Enhanced visible light photo-Fenton-like degradation of tetracyclines by expanded perlite supported FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 floating Z-scheme catalyst
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Yiyang
Wang, Xuejiang
Sun, Qiunan
Yuan, Meng
Sun, Zhenhua
Xia, Siqing
Zhao, Jianfu
coredata.dc:description: In the conventional Fenton system, the relatively low efficiency of Fe (II) regeneration is a significant drawback. To address this shortcoming, a novel floating Z-scheme photo-Fenton catalyst FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP was prepared by a facile dip-calcination method, in which iron and molybdenum oxides with mixed valence states (FeMo3Ox) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) were loaded on the expanded perlite. The removal efficiencies reached the maximum at 98.0%, 93.1% and 97.1% for tetracycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline, respectively, after 60 min dark adsorption and 60 min photo-Fenton process. The aid of dual ion (Fe and Mo) synergy system and photoreduction by Z-scheme photocatalyst enhanced the Fe (II) regeneration, resulting in the excellent performance. Radical scavenger experiment, electron spin resonance spectra (ESR) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were used to confirm the mechanism of free radicals’ formation and Fe/Mo redox cycling. ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 played important roles in the pollutant’s degradation, while the generation of ·O2 - was enhanced due to the floatability in this system. The possible degradation pathways of TC were put forward according to the results of mass spectrum and Orbital-Weighted Fukui Function. Overall, this work provides new insights on the cooperation between iron-based mix oxides and semiconductor in the photo-Fenton system.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Phase and crystal structures of expanded perlite (EP), g-C3N4/EP (CNEP), FeMo3Ox/EP (FMEP), and FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP). (a) XRD patterns of the samples; TEM (b), SAED (c), HRTEM (d–f), HAADF (g) images of FMCNEP and the corresponding elemental mappings of C (h), N (i), O (j), Fe (k), Mo (l).
SEM images of raw expanded perlite (EP) (a, b), g-C3N4/EP (CNEP) (c, d), FeMo3Ox/EP (FMEP) (e, f) and FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP) (g, h).

(a) FTIR spectra patterns of raw expanded perlite (EP), g-C3N4/EP (CNEP), FeMo3Ox/EP (FMEP) and FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP). (b) high resolution XPS spectra of EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP. (c–d) C 1s and N 1s spectra of CNEP and FMCNEP, respectively. (e–f) Fe 2p and Mo 3d spectra of FMEP and FMCNEP, respectively.
UV–vis DRS spectra (a) and the band gaps (b) of raw expanded perlite (EP), g-C3N4/EP (CNEP), FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 (FMEP) and FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP).
PL spectra (a), Photocurrent response (b) and Nyquist plots (c) of FeMo3Ox/EP (FMEP), g-C3N4/EP (CNEP), and FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP).

(a)Tetracycline (TC) removal by expanded perlite (EP), g-C3N4/EP (CNEP), FeMo3Ox/EP (FMEP), FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP) and FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 (FMCN). (b) TC removal by FMCNEP with different Fe/Mo ratios. (c) TC removal by FMCNEP with different ratios between metals and g-C3N4. (d) TC removal by FMCNEP (Fe/Mo = 1/3, total metals: g-C3N4 = 20%) in different reaction conditions. The initial concentration of TC was 25 mg/L, pH = 5, catalyst dosage was 1.33 g/L, and H2O2 concentration = 20 mmol−1.

Tetracycline (TC) removal by FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP) (Fe/Mo = 1/3, total metals: g-C3N4 = 20%) in different initial pH (a), H2O2 dosage (b) and catalyst dosage (c). The initial concentration of TC was 25 mg/L.

Tetracycline (TC) removal by FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP) (Fe/Mo = 1/3, total metals: g-C3N4 = 20%) with different co-existing substances, including anions (a) and HA (b). The concentration of anions was 10 mM, The initial pH = 5, Initial concentration of TC was 25 mg/L, catalyst dosage was 1.33 g/L, and H2O2 concentration = 20 mmol L−1.

(a) Active species trapping experiments and N2 bubbling on TC photo-Fenton degradation by FMCNEP. (b–d) The spectrum of hydroxyl radical, superoxide radical and singlet oxygen at dark and light (Catalyst dosage = 1.33 g/L; H2O2 concentration = 20 mmol L−1; pH = 5.0; DMPO concentration =50 mmol L−1; TEMPO concentration = 50 mmol L−1).
High resolution XPS spectra of Fe 2p and Mo 3d for FMCNEP after reaction under irradiation (a, b) and in dark (c, d).
(a) HRTEM images of photodeposition of PbO2 on FeMo3Ox. (b) HRTEM images of photodeposition of Pt on g-C3N4. (c) Proposed mechanism in the FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 photo-Fenton system.
The pathways of TC degradation in the photo-Fenton system by FMCNEP.
Cycling test of TC Fenton degradation with FMCNEP with the visible light (a), and in dark (b).
BET surface area and pore volume of EP, CNEP and FMCNEP.
Percentage of Fe (II)/Fe (III) and Mo (IV)/Mo (VI) calculated from XPS results.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the conventional Fenton system, the relatively low efficiency of Fe (II) regeneration is a significant drawback. To address this shortcoming, a novel floating Z-scheme photo-Fenton catalyst FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP was prepared by a facile dip-calcination method, in which iron and molybdenum oxides with mixed valence states (FeMo3Ox) and graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) were loaded on the expanded perlite. The removal efficiencies reached the maximum at 98.0%, 93.1% and 97.1% for tetracycline, oxytetracycline and chlortetracycline, respectively, after 60 min dark adsorption and 60 min photo-Fenton process. The aid of dual ion (Fe and Mo) synergy system and photoreduction by Z-scheme photocatalyst enhanced the Fe (II) regeneration, resulting in the excellent performance. Radical scavenger experiment, electron spin resonance spectra (ESR) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were used to confirm the mechanism of free radicals’ formation and Fe/Mo redox cycling. ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 played important roles in the pollutant’s degradation, while the generation of ·O2 - was enhanced due to the floatability in this system. The possible degradation pathways of TC were put forward according to the results of mass spectrum and Orbital-Weighted Fukui Function. Overall, this work provides new insights on the cooperation between iron-based mix oxides and semiconductor in the photo-Fenton system.

ce:section.ce:para: As a series of highly efficient antibiotics, tetracyclines (TCs), including tetracycline (TC), oxytetracycline (OTC) and chlortetracycline (CTC), are commonly used to control bacterial and protozoan infections in livestock and aquaculture industries (Gaballah et al., 2021). However, inappropriate disposal of pharmaceutical wastewater and animal wastes have caused the accumulation of antibiotics in the environment, resulting in a harmful impact on human health and ecological system (Jiang et al., 2021).

Methods for the degradation of TCs in wastewater include biological treatment (G. Zhang et al., 2021), adsorption (Y. Wang et al., 2020), electrochemical treatment (Shi et al., 2021) and advanced oxidation processes (AOPs) (Shao et al., 2020). Fenton technology, as an AOP, has received a great attention in recent years because of its advantages including wide applicability and high efficiency (Cui et al., 2021). Fe2+ can activate hydrogen peroxide solution efficiently and form highly reactive·OH radicals. However, conventional homogeneous Fenton process has many challenges, such as narrow optimal pH range and production of Fe-containing sludge (Miklos et al., 2018). To address these challenges, heterogeneous photo-Fenton technology has been developed. Fe-based oxide catalysts have shown a great potential in the heterogeneous Fenton process. The separability from the waste stream is also an advantage of heterogeneous catalysts (Pignatello et al., 2006). However, the catalytically active species, Fe2+, could be converted to Fe3+ and lose its activity in some materials during the Fenton process (Qin et al., 2015). Therefore, designing composite catalysts with high efficiency and superb cyclability become the important targets of researchers to further enhance the ability of photo-Fenton process in practical applications.

In order to promote the conversion of Fe3+ to Fe2+ in heterogeneous Fenton and Fenton-like processes, building a dual ion (Fe3+/Fe2+ and Mx+/My+) synergy system, such as Fe/Cu (Q. Wang et al., 2018), Fe/Mn (Du et al., 2016), Fe/Co (Xu et al., 2019) or Fe/Nb (Tong et al., 2021) in composite catalysts was proven to be effective in previous studies. In the dual ion synergy systems, Fe3+ can receive electrons from the other metals in low valence and be converted to Fe2+, thus the catalyst ability can be recovered. The standard electrode potential of Mo (IV)/Mo (VI) (0.65 V) is lower than Fe (III)/Fe (II) (0.77 V), which indicates that building a Fe/Mo dual ion synergy system to protect Fe (II) is feasible. Some researchers used MoO2 and MoS2 as co-catalysts in the Fenton and Fenton-like system (Luo et al., 2020; Shen et al., 2019; Xing et al., 2018), under the action of Mo4+ active sites exposed on co-catalysts’ surface, and the efficiency of Fe (III)/Fe (II) cycle was significantly enhanced (Shen et al., 2019), resulting in a better performance in the degradation of organic pollutants.

On the other side, composite catalysts that consisted of Fe-based materials and semi-conductor photocatalysts have shown great potentials in a heterogeneous photo-Fenton system (Zhao et al., 2021). In this system, the photoinduced electrons can be transferred to Fe3+ to enhance the regeneration of Fe2+. In the past decade, graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) was attractive in the photocatalytic organic contaminant degradation due to the narrow bandgap and high stability (Zhang et al., 2019). Various g-C3N4/Fe-based composite materials were used as photo-Fenton catalysts in previous studies (Palanivel and Mani, 2020; Tang et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2020). In addition to promote Fe2+ regeneration, some iron oxides possessing photocatalytic activities, can be combined with g-C3N4 to form heterojunctions. Through forming heterojunctions, the high recombination rate of the photoinduced electrons and holes, which is an important shortcoming for g-C3N4, would decrease, and thus the photocatalytic activity would increase. In addition, using floating photocatalysts to overcome the light energy losses of reflection and water absorption (Loeb et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2017c) in the photocatalytic system were concerned, especially in treating the organic wastewater with high chroma. Immobilizing catalysts onto floating substrates have been proven an efficient way in light management and catalyst recovering (Wang, Xuejiang et al., 2017). Besides, the floating photocatalysts at the gas-liquid interface can react with O2 easily and convert it to the reactive oxygen species (Djellabi et al., 2021). Expanded perlite (Song et al., 2018a, 2018b; Wang et al., 2017a), fly ash cenospheres (Song et al., 2017), and expandable graphite (X. Wang et al., 2018; Wang et al., 2017b; Zhang et al., 2016; Wang et al., 2017a) were employed to be the carriers, due to their excellent floating ability and mechanical and chemical instability.

In this study, a floating z-scheme composite FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 was prepared using a facile dip-calcination method and applied to visible light photo-Fenton-like degradation of tetracyclines. In the photo-Fenton system, it could be inferred theoretically that FeMo3Ox provides Fe2+ to react with H2O2 to form reactive oxygen species degrading organics and Fe/Mo dual ion synergy system enhance the regeneration of Fe2+. Besides, coupling FeMo3Ox and g-C3N4 to form Z-type heterojunction could realize the efficient separation of photoinduced electrons and holes, which can reduce the high-valence metal and provide more active species. Furthermore, the floating properties provided by expanded perlite (EP) allowed for the recycling of the catalyst. The synthesized floating catalysts were characterized and the influencing factors in the photo-Fenton process were investigated. In addition, the mechanisms of photo-Fenton system and pathways of TC degradation were analyzed. As a result, this strategy can provide reference for the design of composite photo-Fenton catalyst with high efficiency and superb cyclability.

Expanded perlite (EP), which is composed mostly of SiO2 and Al2O3, was obtained from Houpu Non-Metallic Mineral Material Co., Ltd. (Zhenzhou, China), and their particle sizes were from 1 mm to 3 mm. The received EP was washed with 0.1 M HNO3 for 30 min (solid: solution = 10 g: 200 mL), and then washed by deionized water until the pH value was neutral. The particles which have good floating performance were selected and dried in a dryer at 60 °C for 24 h. All other chemicals were analytical reagents and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Beijing Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). They were used without any further purification.

The FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP (FMCNEP) was prepared by a facile dip-calcination method. 3 g dicyandiamide and a suitable amount of ferric chloride and ammonium molybdate was added into 20 mL deionized water and mixed to a homogenous state. Then 2 g expanded perlite was added into the solution. The mixture was stirred for 30 min and then dried in an oven at 60 °C for 12 h. Afterwards, the sample was calcinated at 550 °C for 4 h, and the heating rate was 10 °C per minute. According to the ratio among Fe, Mo and dicyandiamide, the samples are labeled as FxMy-z%, where x and y mean the mole rate between Fe and Mo, respectively, and z means the mole rate between total metals and dicyandiamide. For example, F1M3-20% means the dosages of ferric chloride and ammonium molybdate were 1.785 mmol and 5.355 mmol, respectively, as the dosage of dicyandiamide was 35.7 mmol. Because of its best performance in photo-Fenton process, the FMCNEP in this paper generally refers to F1M3-20% in the material characterization.

In addition, CNEP (g-C3N4/EP) and FMEP (FeMo3Ox/EP) were prepared with the same process as the control. However, ferric chloride and ammonium molybdate were not added in the CNEP and dicyandiamide was not added in the FMEP. All prepared materials (CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP) have good floating performance (shown in Fig. S1).

X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was taken with an X-ray powder diffractometer (D8 Advance, Bruker, Germany) using a Cu target radiation source. Transmission electron microscope (TEM), high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns images and energy disperse spectra (EDS) were obtained with JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were obtained with a Gemini SEM 300 (Zeiss, Germany). The Brunauer Emmett Teller (BET) surface area were obtained with a Quadrasorb SI (Quantachrome, America). The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured with a Nicolet 5700 (Thermo Scientific, America). X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) was recorded with a K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific, America) using Al Ka radiation (hv = 1886.6 eV), and the binding energy was calibrated with the C1s peak (284.8 eV). Ultraviolet-visible diffuse reflection spectrum (UV-Vis DRS) was measured with a UV-3600 (Shimazu, Japan), while the bandgap was calculated using the Tauc Plots method (Murphy, 2007). The electron spin resonance (ESR) spectra were conducted on a A300 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) under visible irradiation. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained on a FLS1000 fluorescence spectrometer (Edinburg, Britain). The photocurrent response and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was measured with an electrochemical workstation (CHI-760E, CH Instruments, China).

The photo-Fenton performance of the catalyst was evaluated through the degradation of tetracycline under visible irradiation in a GHX-B photocatalytic reactor (Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co., Ltd). A 500 W Xe lamp was used as light source and 420 nm cutoff filter was used to remove the ultraviolet light. A cooling system was used to eliminate the interference of the heat from the lamp and the reaction temperature was about 25 °C.

Typically, 40 mg of the catalyst and 30 mL of TC solution was added into the quartz reactor and pH was adjusted to 5. The solution was stirred with a magnetic agitator at 180 rpm. Before degradation experiment, the mixture was stirred for 60 min in the dark to reach adsorption equilibrium. After 60 min dark process, the Xe lamp was opened and 20 mM H2O2 was added at the same time. 1.5 mL samples were taken from the reaction solution at appropriate intervals and 0.1 mL 2 M Na2SO3 is used to stop the reaction. 0.45 µm membrane filter was used to remove the photocatalyst.

The concentrations of TC were detected by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC, LC-20A, Shimazu, Japan) with C18 column. The mobile phase was a mixture of 0.01 M aqueous oxalic acid and acetonitrile (70:30, v/v) at a ﬂow rate of 1 mL/min at 25 °C and the detection wavelength was 275 nm. The total organic carbon (TOC) was measured using a TOC analyzer (TOC-L CPN CN200, Shimazu, Japan).

Three-dimensional excitation-emission matrix fluorescence spectra (3D EEMs) were measured with a spectrofluorometer (F-7100, Hitachi, Japan). The leaching of Fe and Mo was evaluated by inductively coupled plasma-optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) (Agilent 720ES, USA). A spectrophotometry (DR6000, Hach, USA) was used to measure the content of Fe2+.

In the reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenge experiment, tert-butyl alcohol (t-BuOH), 1,4-benzoquinone (p-BQ), and L-Tryptophan (L-Trp) were employed as scavengers for ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2, respectively. N2 bubbling was used to evaluate the influence of floating ability in this system.

TC degradation intermediates in the photo-Fenton system were verified by liquid chromatography mass spectrometry (LC-MS, 1290UPLC and QUOF6550, Agilent, America) and the detailed parameters of LC-MS was shown in Text S1.

The density functional theory (DFT) calculations of TC were analyzed by using Gaussian 09 software package with B3LYP/6-311G (d p) level. In addition, Orbital-Weighted Fukui Function, which is calculated with Multiwfn (Lu and Chen, 2012; Pino-Rios et al., 2017), was used to analyze the pathways of TC degradation in the photo-Fenton system.

The phase and crystal structures of EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP were analyzed with XRD and the results are presented in Fig. 1. As shown in Fig. 1a, a typical peak at 27.8° was observed in the CNEP and FMCNEP patterns following the decoration of g-C3N4 on the EP surface, which is assigned to the crystal plane (002) of g-C3N4 (Yang et al., 2013). Peaks at 17.6, 19.8, 25.1, 32.1, 35.9 and 37.0 were observed in the FMEP and FMCNEP patterns, which were corresponded to the (002), (101), (102), (103), (112) and (201) crystal plane of Fe2Mo3O8 (PDF#78-0212), respectively. Moreover, peaks at 26.0 and 37.0 in the FMEP and FMCNEP patterns were corresponded to the (011) and (− 211) crystal plane of MoO2 (PDF#65-5787). The patterns showed that the g-C3N4 was successfully supported on the samples and the Fe2Mo3O8 and MoO2 were two main existing forms of Fe and Mo. As we all know, iron and molybdenum have multiple oxides with different valence state (X. Liu et al., 2020). In the subsequent description, we will refer to these mixed oxides as FeMo3Ox according to the ratio between Fe and Mo.

TEM, HRTEM and SAED were employed to further study the crystal structure and micromorphology of the FMCNEP. As shown in Fig. 1b, tiny Fe2Mo3O8 particles were loaded on the g-C3N4 homogenously, and the size of these particles were about 5 nm. 4 typical diffraction rings of Fe2Mo3O8 ((220), (213), (202) and (102)) were observed in the SAED pattern, which confirmed the formation of Fe2Mo3O8. Furthermore, the HRTEM images (Fig. 1d–f) presented the d-spacing fringes of 0.221 nm and 0.447 nm, which were corresponding to the (011) crystal plane of g-C3N4 (PDF#97-004-1950) and (101) crystal plane of Fe2Mo3O8 (PDF#78-0212), respectively. These results indicated that g-C3N4 and Fe-Mo mix oxides were well combined. To analyze the element compositions, EDS mapping of FMCNEP was measured (Fig. 1g–l). The C, N, O, Fe and Mo element signals were clearly found, indicating the presences of these elements in FMCNEP. As the Fe spectrum shown, some of Fe was aggregated. Fe2Mo3O8 was the mainly existing form of Fe in this material, it could be concluded that Fe2Mo3O8 was deposited at these positions. The signals of Mo were homogeneously distributed, because in this system, Mo existed not only as the Fe2Mo3O8, but also as the MoO2. In addition, the signals of other elements were homogeneously distributed. These results indicated that Fe2Mo3O8 was speared over the surface of g-C3N4. The actual proportion of Fe and Mo was measured by ICP-AES and calculated through the EDS results in three points (Table S1). The ICP results showed that the atomic ratio between Fe and Mo was approximately 1:2.55 and the EDS result was 1: 2.70, which were close to the mole ratio between Fe and Mo precursors (1: 3). Therefore, the mixed oxide was described as FeMo3Ox.

The morphologies and microstructures of prepared composites were explored with SEM. As shown in Fig. 2a, b, the microstructure of raw expanded perlite was clearly uniform and porous, which could make the material float on the water and contribute to the adsorption of photons. The microstructures of CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP, which were impregnated with precursor solution and calcinated at 823 K, are shown in Fig. 2c–h. Compared with the raw EP, it can be seen that the catalysts were successfully deposited on the surface, which resulted in the roughening of the surface. Furthermore, this modification on the surface would not destroy the microstructures of EP, hence the composite materials had great surface area and floatability.

The surface area and pore diameter of raw EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP were measured with BET. The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms of the samples are shown in Fig. S2 and the BET surface area and pore volume are presented in Table 1. The Surface area of EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP were 1.685, 11.061, 2.2948 and 4.559, respectively. The isotherms of all samples were close to the IV type according to the IUPAC (International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry) standard. For the raw EP, the hysteresis loops were close to the H3 type, which was related to its loose structure and slit-like pores (Qiu et al., 2019). With the modification of g-C3N4 and FeMo3Ox, the hysteresis loops of CNEP and FMCNEP changed from H3 to the H4 type, which were related to their mesoporous structure (Gharakhlou and Sarvi, 2017). The specific surface area and pore volume of CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP were larger than that of EP, this might be due to the calcination. However, the specific surface areas and pore volumes of FMEP and FMCNEP were smaller than those of EP and CNEP, respectively, after the doping of Fe and Mo. This could be due to the deposition of mixed oxide on the surface which covered the micropores.

The chemical structures of raw EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP were studied using FT-IR and the spectra are shown in Fig. 3a. The peaks at ~ 1640 and ~ 3500 cm−1 were ascribed to the combination of -OH stretching of hydrogen-bonded and H-O-H bending vibration, which played a positive role in the photocatalyst, because these groups inhibit the recombination of photoinduced electrons and holes (Darkhosh et al., 2019). The peak at around 1060 cm−1 was assigned to the Si-O stretching vibration of EP (Jiang et al., 2017). The spectra of EP and FMEP were similar, the iron and molybdenum oxides have no obvious response in FT-IR. For the FT-IR spectra of CNEP and FMCNEP, the peaks positioned at 1207–1575 cm−1 were recognized as typical stretching vibration of CN and C-N in graphite carbon nitride (Y. Li et al., 2017; Lu et al., 2017), which indicated that g-C3N4 was well combined with EP after treated with precursor solution and calcination.

XPS was used to further explore the chemical composition of the samples and the interaction between metals and the catalysts. Fig. 3b–f shows the full XPS spectra of raw EP, CNEP and FMCNEP. The spectrum of FMCNEP presented the element peaks of C, N, O, Fe and Mo. In the spectra of C 1s, 2 peaks at 284.8, 285.9 and 288.2 eV referred to the presence of CC, CNH2 and NCN bonds (Fu et al., 2019). Meanwhile, for the N 1s spectra, the peaks at 398.5, 400.1, 401.1 referred to the presence of N-CN, N-(C)3 and -NH2, respectively. These peaks were commonly found in the g-C3N4 containing materials, which indicated that g-C3N4 was well loaded on EP. However, both of these peaks in FMCNEP was lower than that in CNEP. This result could be attributed to deposition of Fe and Mo mix oxides, as the formation of g-C3N4 could be affected by the iron oxides (Huang et al., 2021). In the N1s spectra of FMCNEP, 2 peaks at 396.6 and 397.0 eV were found, which referred to the presence of FeN and MoN bonds. It has been reported that the nitrogen atoms in g-C3N4 framework are usually the ideal sites for anchoring metal ions (Gao et al., 2018; C. Li et al., 2017). Correspondingly, FeN and MoN bonds were also found at 708.5 and 229.5 eV in the Fe 2p and Mo 3d spectra of FMCNEP. From the Fe 2p spectra of FMEP and FMCNEP, the two main peaks which refers to 2p1/2 and 2p3/2 were detected. The two peaks in FMEP were divided into 4 peaks, which were ascribed to Fe (III). It can be concluded that the major form of iron in FMEP was Fe (III). However, in the spectrum of FMCNEP, those positioned at 709.9, 722.6, 712.3 and 725.6 eV were ascribed to Fe (II), in which the latter two are satellite peaks. Those positioned at 710.5, 724.1, 716.5 and 730.0 eV were ascribed to Fe (III). Through the calculation of the peak areas, we found that the ratio of Fe (II) and Fe (III) in FMCNEP was 1:3.63. Compare to the FMEP, NH3 produced by dicyandiamide provided a reducing atmosphere in the synthesis process of FMCNEP. From the spectrum of Mo 3d, the two main peaks which referred to 3d 3/2 and 3d 5/2 were detected. The peaks at 230.7 and 233.9 corresponded to Mo (IV) and those positioned at 232.5 and 235.6 corresponded to Mo (VI). Therefore, the ratios of Mo (IV) and Mo (VI) in FMEP and FMCNEP were 1:1.2 and 1:1.84, respectively. Based on the results, Fe and Mo were well doped into the g-C3N4, and the valence of both metals were multiple.

Fig. 4 shows the UV-Vis DRS of raw EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP. The bandgap was calculated using Tauc Plots Method according to Eq. (1):

where α is the adsorption coefficient, h is Planck constant, v is the frequency of light, n is a constant and as for direct band gap semiconductor, n = 1/2, A is a constant, and E
g is the band gap. The raw EP had few adsorptions in visible light. After g-C3N4 loading, a blue shift was observed, and the spectra showed a strong visible-light adsorption. As for FMEP and FMCNEP, the broad light absorption peak increased apparently. As shown in Fig. 4b, the bandgaps of EP, CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP were 3.84, 2.89, 2.92 and 2.05, respectively. The bandgap directly affects the photo response of the materials. Only when the bandgap of semiconductor is smaller than 2.95, the semiconductor can be excited by the light of wavelength longer than 420 nm. The small bandgap of FMCNEP might be due to the introduction of metal energy levels, so that the energy gap of the electronic transition decreased (Kang et al., 2015). The results showed that the optical properties of the floating photocatalysts were proper after decorated.

The recombination extent of free charge carriers was envaulted with Photoluminescence (PL). In general, high fluorescence intensity in PL spectra indicates that the recombination rate of photogenerated electrons and holes is relatively high, and low recombination rate can enhance the reaction efficiency for the photocatalyst. As shown in Fig. 5a, the intensity of FMCNEP was lower than EP and CNEP, which indicated that FMCNEP showed greater potential for photocatalytic reactions. The intensity of FMEP was the lowest, which could be due to the low fluorescence response. In order to compare the photoinduced electrons and holes separation efficiencies and charge transfer resistance of CNEP, FMEP and FMCNEP, photocurrent determination and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were given in Fig. 5b–c. As shown in Fig. 5b, the photocurrent response of FMCNEP was higher than that of FMEP and CNEP, which was indicated that the combination between g-C3N4 and FeMo3Ox enhanced the photo-excited electron migration capability (Yuan et al., 2019). In the EIS results (Fig. 5c), the semicircle radiuses of FMCNEP was smaller than that of CNEP and FMEP, which was an evidence of relatively small electron transfer resistance for FMCNEP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic degradation of TC by FMCNEP
ce:section.ce:para: The catalytic activity of EP, CNEP, FMEP, FMCNEP was evaluated systematically through the degradation of TC under visible light irradiation in the presence of H2O2. The results were shown in Fig. 6a. It could be found that raw EP exhibited little adsorption and degradation ability in the removal of TC. After the loading of g-C3N4, the adsorption and degradation ability increased and 29.86% of TC could be removed after 60 min dark adsorption and 60 min photo-Fenton process. The doping of Fe and Mo played an important role in the Fenton process, the removal efficiency increased significantly after doped with metals. Besides, the performance of FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 without expanded perlite (FMCN) was shown in the Fig. 6a. The concentration of FMCN was 0.307 g/L, which was equal to the content of FMCN in FMCNEP. The adsorption ability of FMCN was lower than CNEP and FMCNEP because of the low surface area. To remove the effect of adsorption, the degradation curves were further analyzed and fitted with pseudo-first-order kinetics (Fig. S3a). The rate constants (k) of TC degradation with EP, CNEP, FMEP, FMCNEP and FMCN were 0.0009, 0.0036, 0.0266, 0.0565, 0.0550 min−1. The catalytic performances of FMCNEP and FMCN were close. EP played an ignorable role in the TC degradation, but provide floating ability for the prepared materials.

The performance of FMCNEP with different metal ratios was shown in Figs. 6b and S3b. The removal efficiency of FMCNEP with different Fe/Mo ratios (1:3, 2:2, 3:1) was 94.8%, 62.9% and 89.4%, respectively. The rate constants (k) of TC degradation with F4M0-CNEP (Fe-CNEP), F0M4-CNEP (Mo-CNEP), F3M1-CNEP, F2M2-CNEP and F1M3-CNEP were 0.0060, 0.0219, 0.0324, 0.0127, 0.0565 min−1, respectively. Single metal can improve the degradation slightly because H2O2 can be activated by transition metals (Z. Zhang et al., 2021). In this study, 39.8% and 80.1% of TC could be removed by Fe-doped CNEP and Mo-doped CNEP, respectively. Due to the cooperation of the doped Fe and Mo, the catalytic effectiveness increased significantly. As discussed above, the mole ratio between Fe and Mo at 1:3 showed the best degradation efficiency on TC removal. Meanwhile, compared to the CNEP and FMCNEP, 78.8% of TC could be removed by FMEP, indicating that metals had a greater contribution than g-C3N4.

Figs. 6c and S3c exhibited the effect of different ratios between total metals and g-C3N4 on the TC removal. After 1 h dark reaction and 1 h photo-Fenton process, 63.8%, 71.0%, 96.9% and 81.1% of TC was removed by F1M3-5%, F1M3-10%, F1M3-20% and F1M3-30%, respectively. With the metal content increased in the composite, the removal efficiency first rose and then dropped. When the ratio was smaller than 20%, more active sites on the catalyst surface contributes to the photo-Fenton reaction. However, excess metals would block the active sites which caused the decrease of removal (Xing et al., 2021). As discussed above, the ratio between total metals and g-C3N4 at 20% showed the best degradation efficiency on TC removal.

The degradation curves of TC under different reaction systems are shown in Fig. 6d. Only 19.2% of TC could be removed by photocatalyst without H2O2, indicating the photocatalytic performance of FMCNEP was limited. As for photoactivation of H2O2 without catalysts, only 29.5% of TC could be removed. 97.4% and 96.1% of TC were removed by photo-Fenton and dark Fenton, respectively. The TC degradation in Vis/H2O2/FMCNEP system and H2O2/FMCNEP could be fitted well with pseudo-first-order kinetics (R2 > 0.99) (Fig. S3d). The rate constant (k) of TC degradation in the Vis/H2O2/FMCNEP system was found to be 1.13 times of that in the H2O2/FMCNEP system, with values of 0.05694 and 0.05043 min−1. Fenton in visible radiation was slightly better than Fenton in dark. These results indicated that the Fenton process play an important role in this system, instead of simple photocatalysis or photoactivation of H2O2. Meanwhile, the degradation curves of OTC and CTC in the photo-Fenton system were shown in Fig. S4 and 93.1% of OTC and 97.1% of CTC were removed after 1 h. The results showed that this method has general applicability to other TCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of pH, H2O2 concentration and catalyst dosage on tetracycline removal
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of parameters of photo-Fenton by FMCNEP were investigated in batch experiment. Fig. 7a exhibits TC degradation in different initial pH values. There was no significant disparity in adsorption in dark with the initial pH ranging from 3% to 9% and 21.6% to 28.5% of TC could be adsorbed by FMCNEP in 1 h. Lower pH was favorable for adsorption and 42.0% of TC could be adsorbed when pH = 2. For the photo-Fenton process, it has been recognized that pH played an important role in the Fenton and Fenton-like process (Chen et al., 2021). The optimal pH was 5% and 97.0% of TC could be removed at such a pH value. The relatively low degradation at pH = 9 should be caused by the oxidizability decrease of the hydroxyl radicals. The redox potential of hydroxyl radicals is + 2.8 V in the acidic solution and + 1.5 V in the basic solution.

Fig. 7b exhibits the TC degradation with different H2O2 dosages. With the increasing amount of H2O2 from 5 mM to 20 mM, the removal efficiency increased rapidly from 51.1% to 97.5%. However, while the dosage of H2O2 reached 30 mM, the removal efficiency showed a slight decrease. H2O2 could react with the active sites on the catalyst surface, hence the increasing of oxidant within range of 5–20 mM could promote the degradation of TC. However excessive H2O2 inhibited the Fenton process. Excessive H2O2 in the system could cause a ROS scavenging effect and lead to the decrease of hydroxyl radicals (Huang et al., 2020). Similar results in antibiotics degradation were obtained by other researchers (Wan and Wang, 2017; Zha et al., 2014).

It has been recognized that catalyst dosage is an important factor in the photo-Fenton system, because the active sites on the catalyst is strongly dependent on the catalyst dosage. Fig. 7c exhibits the influence of catalyst dosage on TC degradation. With the increasing dosage of FMCNEP from 0.33 g/L to 1.33 g/L, the total removal efficiency including adsorption and degradation increased rapidly from 78.0% to 98.0%. When the catalyst dosage increased to 1.67 g/L, the removal efficiency decreased. As for adsorption, increasing catalyst dosage causes the increase of adsorption due to the rise of adsorption sites. However, as for the degradation, excessive catalysts not only failed to improve, but decreased slightly. This might be due to the blocking of light or active sites on the FMCNEP surface. Excess catalysts affected the adsorption of photon, thus the removal efficiency decreased (J. Jiang et al., 2019).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of co-existing substances on tetracycline removal
ce:section.ce:para: Anions, cations and natural organic matters exist widely in the actual water. These coexisting substances may affect the performance of adsorption and photo-Fenton process. To investigate the influence of anions, 10 mM of Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, HCO3 - and H2PO4 - was added into the photo-Fenton system ( Fig. 8a). For dark adsorption, NO3 - and H2PO4 - inhibited the adsorption slightly. This might be due to the competition between the anions and TC. Other anions had no significant influence in the adsorption process. As for photo-Fenton process, HCO3 - and H2PO4 - could inhibit the degradation of TC significantly. The results were similar to a previous study which concluded that carbonate ion and phosphate ion could work as scavenger for hydroxyl radicals (Jiang et al., 2020). Besides, the H2PO4 - could react with hydroxyl radicals to form the phosphate radicals, hence the oxidability decreased (Nie et al., 2018). Fig. S5 showed the influence of cations in the TC degradation. Four different kinds of common cations including Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and K+ had nearly no effect on the TC degradation, which is similar to other studies’ findings (Jiang et al., 2020).

Humic acid (HA) exists widely in the actual water and may cause self-inhibition or light attenuation in advanced oxidation processes (Pozdnyakov et al., 2020). However, in this system, as shown in Fig. 8b, 5 mg/L of HA had nearly no effect on TC degradation. This might be due to the promotion of photolysis by the excited triplet state of humic acid (3HA*). When the concentration of HA was larger than 10 mg/L, both the adsorption process and photo-Fenton process was inhibited. In order to elucidate the influence of HA in the TC photo-Fenton degradation, 3D EEMs was employed and the results were shown in Fig. S6. Fluorescence characteristic depends on the structure and functional groups of contaminants (J. Wang et al., 2018). TC molecules have no significant fluorescence signals due to the electro-withdrawing groups of TC molecules (Huang et al., 2020). However, after adding the catalyst, a fluorescence peak at wavelength of Ex/Em = 365–385/515–535 (Peak I) appeared, which represents a humic acid-like organics. This might be due to the self-degradation of TC or metal complexes. This finding was similar to some other studies (X. Zhang et al., 2021). Peak II at wavelength of Ex/Em = 260–270/480–500 was corresponding to HA and the intensity of Peak II was positively correlated to the HA concentration. However, Peak II could also be found in the blank group, which might be due to other fulvic acids-like substances converted from TC (Wang et al., 2016). When H2O2 was added under visible irradiation, Peak I decreased rapidly and disappeared after 30 min when the concentration of HA is 0 and 5 mg/L. Nevertheless, when the concentration of HA reached 10 mg/L, the velocity of the disappearance of Peak I was lower, and Peak I could be clearly found when the reaction time was 30 min. This result indicated that the high concentration of HA (> 10 mg/L) would inhibit the degradation of TC, which was well fitted with the degradation test. Peak II also decreased with the increase of reaction time, which indicated that HA and the fulvic acids-like substances could also be removed by the photo-Fenton system. This was due to the strong and nonselective oxidizability of the active species in this system. As Peaks I and II decreased, Peak III (Ex/Em = 330–340/430–440) and Peak IV(Ex/Em = 250–260/410–420) increased. This stated the fact that two other humic acid-like organics were newly generated (Huang et al., 2019). The trend of the increase of Peaks III and IV were also the same as the TC degradation, the intensities of Peaks III and IV were similar when the concentration of HA was 0 and 5 mg/L, and the intensities were relatively lower when the concentration of HA was 10 mg/L.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Scavenging and identification of free radicals in TC degradation by FMCNEP
ce:section.ce:para: The radical scavenger experiments were employed to investigate the main reaction oxidation species in photo-Fenton system ( Fig. 9a). t-BuOH, p-BQ, and L-Trp were selected as the scavengers of ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 (Huang et al., 2020; Song et al., 2017). The degradation efficiencies decreased significantly after adding t-BuOH, p-BQ or L-Trp, suggesting that ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 participated in the TC degradation process. Among these, 1O2 and ·OH played important roles in the process and the degradation efficiencies declined to the 47.6% and 74.5% after the introduction of t-BuOH and L-Trp, respectively. The photo-Fenton catalyst was grinded up to lose its floating ability to investigate the difference between suspension system and floating system. As the experimental results shown, the TC removal efficiency in suspension system was slightly lower than that of floating system. As the previous study showed, floating materials were able to receive O2 from air, and O2 on the material surface could improve the separation of charge carriers and act as a source of ·O2 - and other reactive oxygen species (Herrmann, 2005). In order to further reveal the effect of ·O2 - in floating system, N2 bubbling experiments were conducted, N2 bubbling could eliminate the dissolved oxygen in the aqueous solution and then inhibit the formation of reaction ·O2 - (Asadzadeh-Khaneghah et al., 2019). The results showed that N2 bubbling inhibited the degradation process in suspension system but played an ironable role in the floating system. The results indicated that due to the floatability of the catalyst, it could react with oxygen in the air and make the formation of ·O2 - more efficiently.

ESR was employed to identify the free radicals in the photo-Fenton process. As shown in Fig. 9b–d, in the dark adsorption process, no significant signals were obtained. After adding H2O2 under visible irradiation, four peaks of 1:2:2:1 proportion were detected in the ·OH spectra and the intensities increased with the prolonging time. As shown in Fig. 9c–d, superoxide radical and singlet oxygen showed a similar trend (red line) with ·OH. These results illustrated that ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 were generated after H2O2 added under the visible irradiation. Meanwhile, the spectra of all three radicals in the dark conditions were also collected and shown as black lines. Compared to the light reaction, the intensities of ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 in dark conditions were significantly lower. This illustrated that the light played an important role in the formation of ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2. In order to further explain the formation mechanism of ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2, and reveal the role of g-C3N4, the ESR tests of FMEP/H2O2 systems were conducted and shown as blue lines in the Fig. S7. Compare to the FMCNEP/ H2O2 system, The ·OH and ·O2 - intensities of FMEP in darkness were higher than those of FMCNEP in darkness. However, the 1O2 intensity of FMEP was much smaller. Some previous studies pointed out that 1O2 could be generated by the disproportionation reaction of ·OH (Shen et al., 2019) and the conversion of ·O2 - on the g-C3N4 surface (Z. Wang et al., 2020). The reason for the low intensities of ·OH and ·O2 - in FMCNEP/ H2O2 system could be ascribed to the generation of 1O2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Catalytic mechanism of photo-Fenton system by FMCNEP
ce:section.ce:para:
To verify the catalytic process on the catalyst surface, the FMCNEP after 60 min Fenton process with and without visible irradiation were investigated by XPS ( Fig. 10). Through calculating the peak areas, the percentage of Fe (II)/Fe (III) and Mo (IV)/Mo (VI) were shown in Table 2. As we all known, Fe (II) is the main catalytic active matter in the common Fenton system, it can react with H2O2 to form ·OH and then converted to Fe (III) (Eq. (2)). The catalytic activity of Fe (III) is much lower than Fe (II) and Fe (III) can combine with OH- and form unwanted Fe-containing sludge, which causes secondly pollution. Although Fe (III) can react with H2O2 to form Fe (II) (Eqs. (3) and (4)), the reaction efficiency is relatively low, resulting in the consumption of Fe (II) and accumulation of Fe (III) (Zhu et al., 2019). However, in this study, the ratio of Fe (II) in the catalyst increased no matter with or without the visible light. Meanwhile, the ratio of Mo (IV) decreased after the reaction which confirmed the regeneration of Fe (II) by the reduction reaction of Mo (IV) (Eq. (5)). As some studies reported, the Mo (VI) site on the catalysts surface could react with Fe3+ and convert Fe3+ to Fe 2+ (Xing et al., 2018). Subsequently, the Mo (VI) can react with H2O2 then back to Mo (IV) (Eq. (6)), thus forming a redox cycle. In this system, the efficiency of Fe (II)/Fe (III) catalytic cycling was enhanced due to the aid of Mo (IV)/Mo (VI). The low consumption of Fe (II) and low production of Fe-containing sludge was prospective to meet the tendency of green chemistry.

On the other side, the participation of visible irradiation was envaulted by comparing the XPS results between photo-Fenton and dark Fenton. As the results shown, the ratios of both two low-valence metals (Fe (II) and Mo (IV)) were higher under irradiation than those in dark. In order to investigate the detailed mechanism of this process, the valence bands of FeMo3Ox and C3N4 were measured with XPS (Fig. S8). The conduction band was calculated with valence band and the bandgap, and the results of FeMo3Ox and C3N4 were shown in Fig. 11. The VB of FeMo3Ox and g-C3N4 were about 2.93 eV and 1.72 eV, respectively, and the CB of FeMo3Ox and g-C3N4 were − 0.02 eV and − 1.17 eV, respectively. Due to the narrow bandgap, FeMo3Ox and C3N4 were excited by the visible irradiation and generated photogenerated electron-hole pairs (Eq. (7)). There are two possible charge carriers transfer way based on the position of FeMo3Ox and C3N4 energy levels including the traditional type-II and the Z-scheme (Q. Liu et al., 2020; Palanivel and Mani, 2020; Zhang et al., 2020). For the type-II mechanism, the photogenerated electrons in CB of g-C3N4 would transfer to the CB of FeMo3Ox, the photogenerated holes in VB of FeMo3Ox would transfer to the VB of g-C3N4. However, the electrons in the CB of FeMo3Ox could not reduce the O2 to ·O2 -, because the standard redox potential of O2/·O2 is − 0.33 eV, which is more negative than the CB of FeMo3Ox (− 0.02 V). As the ESR results shown, ·O2 - can be found only in the light reaction, indicated that ·O2 - was formed by the photocatalytic process. Therefore, the pathways of charge carriers should be the Z-scheme type. In order to further certificate the transfer routes of photoinduced electrons and holes, PbO2 and Pt deposition experiments were employed. PbO2 obtained from Pb2+ could be deposited on the sites of holes and Pt nanoparticles obtained from H2PtCl6 could be deposited by accepting electrons (W. Jiang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2021; Wei et al., 2021). The detailed experiments information was shown in Text S2. As the Fig. 11a shown, after the photooxidation of Pb2+ on the FMCNEP, the lattice fringes of 0.247 nm and 0.355 nm, which could be assigned to the (200) facet of PbO2 (PDF# 37-0517) and (102) facet of Fe2Mo3O8 (PDF#78-0212), were observed. Meanwhile, Pt nanoparticles obtained from H2PtCl6 were deposited over g-C3N4. The lattice fringes of 0.240 nm and 0.196 nm, which could be assigned to the (001) facet of g-C3N4 (PDF#97-004-1950) and (200) facet of Pt (PDF#70-2057) were observed in the Fig. 11b. The PbO2 and Pt deposition results show that the photo-generated electrons were accumulated in g-C3N4 and holes were accumulated in FeMo3Ox, Z-scheme transfer route was confirmed. The photogenerated electrons in the CB of FeMo3Ox would transfer to the VB of g-C3N4 and then combined with the holes in the VB of g-C3N4. The charge carriers were well separated, resulting in a better separation of charge carriers and a higher redox ability. As the VB of FeMo3Ox (2.93 eV) was more positive than the standard redox potential of H2O/·OH (2.4 eV), it could convert H2O to ·OH directly (Eq. (8)). As for the electrons in CB of g-C3N4, due to the negative position of CB, the electrons could convert O2 to ·O2 - (Eq. (9)) and reduce the Fe (III) and Mo (VI) to Fe (II) and Mo (IV) (Eqs. (10)–(11)), respectively. This was the reason of higher ratios of two low-valence metals (Fe (II) and Mo (IV)) in the material after photo-Fenton reaction than in dark. Besides, the 1O2 could be generated on the Mo surface by the disproportionation reaction of ·OH (Shen et al., 2019) (Eq. (12)) and the combination of ·O2 - with H2O (Eq. (13)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of intermediate products and the proposed reaction pathway
ce:section.ce:para: Total organic carbon measurement was used to analyze the mineralization of TC through photo-Fenton process. As displayed in Fig. S9, the TOC removal in Vis/FMCNEP/H2O2 system was better than those in Vis/FMEP/H2O2 and in Vis/CNEP/H2O2 system. However, although 98% of TC could be removed after 60 min photo-Fenton process, only 47.4% of TOC was removed in Vis/FMCNEP/H2O2 system after 120 min, indicating that the mineralization rate was relatively low in this system (Chen et al., 2017). Therefore, varied intermediate products were generated in the photo-Fenton system. LC-MS was employed to identify the intermediate products and the majority of intermediate product was shown in the Fig. S10 (P2–P9).

To investigate the mechanism that produced these identified intermediate products, Orbital-weighted Fukui Function was used to analyze the pathway of TC in photo-Fenton system. The Orbital-weighted Fukui function of TC was shown in Table S2 and the atomic numbering of TC used for OW-Fukui function analysis and discussion of reaction sites was shown in Fig. S11. According to Fukui function, a high OW f+ value indicates that an atom is more likely to be attacked by ·O2 - as nucleophilic reaction, a high OW f- value indicates that the atom is more likely to occur electrophilic reaction including being attacked by ·OH and protonation. Moreover, a high OW f0 value indicates that an atom is more likely to occur free radical reaction (Pino-Rios et al., 2017).

In the TC molecule (P1), O22 and O26 have a high OW f- value, demonstrating the potential for protonation. H+ can combine with the oxygen atoms and then dehydrate to form double bond, which is shown in Fig. 12 and marked with blue. This process has been widely reported in the TC degradation (Han et al., 2020; K. Liu et al., 2020). P1.1 and P1.2 (m/z = 427 and 409) were the intermediate products that lose one and two molecules of water from TC, respectively. More than that, by comparing the mass-to-charge ratio of other intermediate products, it could be found that the dehydration could occur in other intermediate products which have similar structure. Thus, the dehydration products were marked with Px. 1 and Px. 2 for those products which lose one or two molecules of water.

C2, N19 and O20, also have high OW f- value, and these atoms were more likely to occur electrophilic reaction. Two possible pathways were shown in Fig. 12 (marked with green). For the Pathway I, ·OH could attack the C2 directly and the amide group (-NHCO) was then removed, and TC converted to P2 (m/z = 402). The similar products were observed in previous reports (Huang et al., 2019). As for the Pathway II, with the assistance of protonation, a molecular rearrangement reaction occurred in the amide group, then the carbonyl group was removed and P3 (m/z = 417) generated. Correspondingly, the dehydration products of P2 and P3 (P2.1, P2.2, P3.1 and P3.2) which have the mass-to-charge ratio of 384, 366, 399 and 381 were observed in the MS results, and these results agreed with the pathways.

Pathway III was ring cleavage reactions. As the strong oxidants, ·O2 - and 1O2 were found in the photo-Fenton system, the atoms on the rings might be attacked by the strong oxidants and then open the ring (Chen et al., 2019). Two ring cleavage products, with the same mass-to-charge ratio (m/z = 461) were found in the MS results, as well as their dehydration products. According to the OW Fukui function calculation, C1 and C11 have high OW f+ and OW f0 values, and these sites are more likely to be attacked by ·O2 - and 1O2. Therefore, the ring cleavage reactions were more likely to occur in the C1 and C11 and then the intermediate product P4 and P5 were produced. Moreover, the ring cleavage products could be further oxidized in the system. P4 was divided to P6 (m/z = 165) and P7 (m/z = 281) after the rupture of C9–C14 bond. On the other hand, P8 and P9 were produced due to the rupture of C2–C3 and hydrolysis from P5. Correspondingly, the dehydration products of P7 and P8 were also found. Finally, these intermediate products from all three pathways were continually degraded into smaller molecules and then mineralized into NH3, H2O and CO2.

The stability and reusability of the catalyst are important factors in practical applications. In order to investigate the stability and reusability of FMCNEP in this system, 5 cycles experiments of Fenton with and without visible irradiation were employed. As shown in Fig. 13, the adsorption performance of FMCNEP significantly decreased. This might be due to the decreased adsorption sites in continuous operation. 60 min Fenton process cannot remove all organics adsorbed on the catalyst, so the adsorption sites decreased. For photo-Fenton degradation, there was a slight decrease for the removal efficiency after each cycle. But after 5 cycles, the removal efficiency was still higher than 85%. As for Fenton process in the dark, the decrease in removal efficiency was more significant than that in photo-Fenton, after 5 cycles, the removal efficiency was only 64.0%. These results agree with the mechanism proposed above. According to the XPS data after reaction, visible irradiation could help two metals transfer to the low valence state and obtain a long-term activity in catalyst reaction. Meanwhile, the experiment of Fe and Mo dissolution was carried out. As shown in Fig. S12a–b, the amount of leaching total Fe, Fe2+ and Mo after Cycle 1 were ~ 2.06, ~ 0.40 and ~ 6.15 mg/L (both less than 2.5% in FMCNEP) in the Vis/FMCNEP/H2O2 system and ~ 1.95, ~ 0.30 and ~ 6.10 mg/L in the FMCNEP/H2O2 system, respectively. With the prolonging of cycle experiment, the leaching amount decreased. The leaching of metals in two system were close. These results confirmed the stability and recycling of the catalyst in the photo-Fenton system. The contribution of leached metals to TC degradation was shown in Fig. S13, by analyzing the degradation efficiency in a homogeneous Vis/Fe3+/Fe2+/Mo/H2O2 system, only 28.7% of TC was remove. The results indicated that the degradation process was mainly dominated by heterogeneous reaction.

In this work, the floating Z-scheme FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4/EP catalyst was successfully prepared using a facile dip-calcination method to remove pollutants in wastewater. The FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 exhibited 98.0% degradation efficiency for TC in the photo-Fenton system, which was obviously higher than FeMo3Ox/EP and g-C3N4/EP. Meanwhile, 93.1% of OTC and 97.1% of CTC could also be removed in this system. The excellent performance of FeMo3Ox/g-C3N4 was mainly ascribed to the facilitation effect in the Fe (II) regeneration by the dual ion (Fe (II)/Fe (III) and Mo (IV)/Mo (VI)) synergy system and photogenerated charge carriers produced by Z-scheme photocatalyst. Abundant ROSs including ·OH, ·O2 - and 1O2 were generated from the photo-Fenton process and then degraded TC to small molecule substances. Floating catalyst can react with oxygen in the air more efficiently to produce ·O2 -, while providing separability. In addition, the possible degradation pathways of TC were proposed. As for coexisting substance, Normal ions including Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 -, Na+, Ca2+, Mg2+ and K+ (10 mM) and low concentration of humic acid (5 mg/L) played ignorable roles in this system, while HCO3 -, H2PO4 - and high concentration of HA would inhibit the degradation of TC in this system. All in all, this work provided a deep understanding for the synergy of duplex metal and semiconductor in the photo-Fenton system.

Yiyang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Visualization, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xuejiang Wang: Resources, Supervision, Data curation, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Qiunan Sun: Software, Writing – review & editing. Meng Yuan: Formal analysis. Zhenhua Sun: Writing – review & editing. Siqing Xia: Writing – review & editing. Jianfu Zhao: Resources, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.
